landmarks.name: Budj Bim Cultural Landscape
landmarks.shortDescription: A 6,600-year-old aquaculture system built by the Gunditjmara people — older than the Egyptian pyramids.
landmarks.fullStory:
Budj Bim, in south-west Victoria, is one of Australia's most remarkable Aboriginal heritage sites and a UNESCO World Heritage-listed cultural landscape. The Gunditjmara people engineered an extraordinary aquaculture system here more than 6,600 years ago, making it one of the oldest known aquaculture systems in the world — older than the pyramids of Egypt.

The Gunditjmara used lava flows from the dormant Budj Bim volcano to construct a complex network of stone channels, weirs, and holding ponds to trap, store, and harvest kooyang (short-finned eels). These engineered waterways stretched across the landscape, allowing the Gunditjmara to manage eel populations year-round and sustain large, permanent settlements.

This challenges the old assumption that Aboriginal Australians were only nomadic hunter-gatherers. The Gunditjmara built stone dwellings, managed food resources through engineering, and lived in villages — a settled way of life sustained by deep knowledge of the land.

Today, the Gunditjmara people continue to care for Budj Bim. The site's World Heritage listing in 2019 recognised not just its archaeological importance, but the living cultural connection that the Gunditjmara maintain with this extraordinary place.

landmarks.name: Mount Eccles / Budj Bim National Park
landmarks.shortDescription: A dormant volcano on Gunditjmara country whose lava flows shaped one of the world's oldest aquaculture landscapes.
landmarks.fullStory:
Mount Eccles, now known as Budj Bim National Park, sits at the heart of the volcanic landscape that the Gunditjmara people transformed into a vast aquaculture system. The dormant volcano and its surrounding lava flows created the natural foundations — stone, channels, and wetlands — that the Gunditjmara engineered over thousands of years.

The crater lake at the summit, Lake Surprise, is a sacred site. The volcanic terrain provided the basalt stone that the Gunditjmara shaped into channel walls, weirs, and house foundations. Lava tubes, caves, and rock formations throughout the park hold cultural significance and were used for shelter, ceremony, and food storage.

The park contains evidence of permanent stone dwellings — circular foundations that once supported bark and timber structures. These villages, sustained by the eel aquaculture system, supported one of the highest population densities of any Aboriginal community in pre-contact Australia.

Mount Eccles / Budj Bim National Park is jointly managed by the Gunditjmara people and Parks Victoria, ensuring that the deep cultural knowledge of this landscape continues to guide its care and interpretation.

landmarks.name: Tyrendarra Indigenous Protected Area
landmarks.shortDescription: A Gunditjmara-managed landscape preserving ancient stone house foundations and eel harvesting channels.
landmarks.fullStory:
Tyrendarra Indigenous Protected Area, on the south-west Victorian coast near Portland, protects a landscape of immense cultural significance to the Gunditjmara people. The area contains some of the best-preserved evidence of Aboriginal stone house foundations and eel harvesting infrastructure in Australia.

Archaeological surveys at Tyrendarra have revealed the remains of circular stone dwellings, arranged in clusters that suggest organised village settlements. Alongside these houses, the engineered channels and weirs of the Gunditjmara aquaculture system are clearly visible — stone-lined waterways designed to direct eels into traps during their seasonal migrations.

The coastal environment at Tyrendarra also provided shellfish, fish, and seabirds, while the inland wetlands supported plant foods and freshwater species. The Gunditjmara managed this diverse landscape through controlled burning and water management, maintaining its productivity across seasons.

Declared an Indigenous Protected Area in 2015, Tyrendarra is managed by the Gunditjmara people through the Winda-Mara Aboriginal Corporation. It represents a powerful assertion of Aboriginal land management and custodianship — ancient knowledge applied to contemporary conservation.

landmarks.name: Lake Condah Mission
landmarks.shortDescription: A place of resilience where the Gunditjmara people endured the mission era and kept culture alive.
landmarks.fullStory:
Lake Condah Mission, established in 1867 near the shores of Lake Condah (Tae Rak) in south-west Victoria, was one of several missions set up to confine and control Aboriginal people during the colonial period. The Gunditjmara and other Aboriginal people were moved here under government policy, separated from their traditional lands and subjected to strict rules about language, movement, and cultural practice.

Despite the hardship and restrictions, the Gunditjmara people at Lake Condah found ways to maintain their culture, language, and connection to country. They continued to practise traditions, pass on knowledge to younger generations, and care for the land around the mission as they had for thousands of years.

The mission was closed in 1913, and the land was taken for soldier settlements. After decades of campaigning, the Gunditjmara won back ownership of parts of their traditional lands through a landmark native title determination in 2007.

Today, the remains of the mission — stone buildings, a chimney, and other structures — stand as a powerful reminder of what Aboriginal people endured and survived. Lake Condah is both a place of sorrow and a place of strength, showing the resilience of Aboriginal culture in the face of enormous loss.

landmarks.name: Kurtonitj
landmarks.shortDescription: A Gunditjmara homeland site where stone house ruins and cultural landscape connect past and present.
landmarks.fullStory:
Kurtonitj is a Gunditjmara cultural site in south-west Victoria, part of the broader volcanic landscape that encompasses Budj Bim, Lake Condah, and Tyrendarra. The site preserves evidence of stone house foundations and cultural features that demonstrate the settled, complex society the Gunditjmara maintained for thousands of years.

The name Kurtonitj reflects the Gunditjmara language and the deep connection between place, language, and identity. Each named site across Gunditjmara country carries stories, responsibilities, and knowledge — a living map of culture embedded in the landscape.

Kurtonitj sits within the broader cultural landscape that was recognised for World Heritage listing. The interconnected network of sites across Gunditjmara country — from volcanic craters to engineered waterways to village ruins — tells a story of sophisticated land management and permanent settlement that challenges outdated assumptions about Aboriginal life.

The Gunditjmara people continue to care for Kurtonitj and the surrounding country, drawing on the knowledge passed down through generations while working with contemporary land management practices to protect this irreplaceable heritage.

landmarks.name: Brambuk — The National Park & Cultural Centre
landmarks.shortDescription: A living cultural centre in the Grampians where Aboriginal culture is shared and celebrated.
landmarks.fullStory:
Brambuk — The National Park and Cultural Centre sits at the entrance to the Grampians (Gariwerd) National Park in western Victoria. It is a place where the living culture of the Jardwadjali and Djab Wurrung peoples is shared with visitors from around the world.

The centre's distinctive building was designed to reflect Aboriginal cultural themes, with its sweeping roofline inspired by the shape of a cockatoo in flight. Inside, visitors can learn about the Dreaming stories of Gariwerd, the significance of the rock art in the mountains, and the deep connection between the Aboriginal peoples and their country.

Brambuk offers cultural programs including guided walks to rock art sites, boomerang throwing, didgeridoo playing, and bush tucker tastings. These programs are led by Aboriginal guides who share their knowledge and stories directly with visitors, keeping culture alive through personal connection.

The centre is owned and managed by Aboriginal communities, making it a powerful example of self-determination and cultural pride. Brambuk demonstrates that Aboriginal culture is not something from the past — it is a living, breathing tradition that continues to grow and adapt while staying connected to ancient roots.

landmarks.name: Bunjil's Shelter
landmarks.shortDescription: A sacred rock shelter with a painting of Bunjil, the creator spirit and wedge-tailed eagle.
landmarks.fullStory:
Bunjil's Shelter, near Stawell in western Victoria, is one of the most culturally significant Aboriginal sites in south-eastern Australia. The shelter contains a painting of Bunjil, the great creator spirit of the Kulin Nation peoples, depicted as a wedge-tailed eagle accompanied by his two helpers, Djurt-djurt (the nankeen kestrel).

Bunjil is central to the Dreaming of the Kulin Nation. He is believed to have created the mountains, rivers, animals, and people. After shaping the world, Bunjil rose into the sky where he watches over his creation. The painting at this shelter is one of the most important representations of Bunjil and has been a place of spiritual significance for thousands of years.

The rock art at Bunjil's Shelter was created using red ochre, and the figures are painted in a distinctive style. The shelter itself is a natural sandstone overhang that has protected the painting from the elements for millennia.

Bunjil's Shelter is a registered Aboriginal cultural heritage place and is protected under Victorian law. Visitors can view the painting from a viewing platform, but the shelter itself is sacred and must be treated with the deepest respect.

landmarks.name: Gulgurn Manja Shelter
landmarks.shortDescription: A Grampians rock shelter with over 100 hand stencils — one of the richest hand-art sites in south-east Australia.
landmarks.fullStory:
Gulgurn Manja, meaning 'hands of young people', is a spectacular rock art shelter in the northern Grampians (Gariwerd). The site contains more than 100 hand stencils — one of the largest collections of hand art at any single site in south-eastern Australia.

The hand stencils were created by placing a hand against the rock wall and blowing a mouthful of ochre paint around it, leaving a negative image. The stencils come in many sizes, from tiny children's hands to large adult prints, suggesting that people of all ages participated in creating art at this site over many generations.

Hand stencils are among the most personal forms of rock art. Each print is unique — a direct physical impression of an individual who stood in that shelter hundreds or thousands of years ago. The layering of prints at Gulgurn Manja, with newer stencils overlapping older ones, creates a powerful visual record of continuous cultural practice across deep time.

The site is managed by Parks Victoria with guidance from the Jardwadjali and Djab Wurrung peoples. Visitors can view the shelter from a boardwalk, but touching the rock art is prohibited to prevent further deterioration of these irreplaceable images.

landmarks.name: Ngamadjidj Shelter
landmarks.shortDescription: A rock shelter in the Grampians with distinctive white-painted figures depicting spirit beings.
landmarks.fullStory:
Ngamadjidj Shelter, in the northern Grampians (Gariwerd), contains a remarkable collection of rock paintings executed in white clay or pipeclay. The figures depict what are believed to be spirit beings — elongated, ghost-like forms that appear to float across the rock surface.

The white figures at Ngamadjidj are distinctive in the Grampians rock art tradition, where most paintings use red, yellow, or orange ochre. The choice of white pigment and the ethereal style of the figures suggest that these paintings may have held particular spiritual significance, perhaps representing ancestral spirits or beings from the Dreaming.

The name Ngamadjidj relates to the white figures themselves, reflecting the Aboriginal practice of naming places after their most significant cultural features. The shelter provided protection from weather and was likely used as a campsite as well as a place for ceremony and art.

Ngamadjidj is one of over 80 per cent of Victoria's known rock art sites located within Gariwerd. Together, these sites form one of the richest collections of Aboriginal rock art in south-eastern Australia, spanning over 20,000 years of artistic and cultural expression.

landmarks.name: Billimina Shelter
landmarks.shortDescription: A Grampians rock shelter with some of the oldest dated rock art in south-eastern Australia.
landmarks.fullStory:
Billimina Shelter, in the southern Grampians (Gariwerd), is one of the most archaeologically significant Aboriginal sites in Victoria. Excavations at the shelter have yielded evidence of human occupation stretching back over 20,000 years, and the rock art on its walls includes some of the oldest dated paintings in south-eastern Australia.

The art at Billimina includes hand stencils, animal figures, and geometric designs painted in red, yellow, and white ochre. The shelter floor has produced stone tools, charcoal from cooking fires, and other artefacts that paint a detailed picture of how Aboriginal people lived in Gariwerd across thousands of years, through ice ages and changing climates.

Billimina demonstrates the extraordinary continuity of Aboriginal culture. People were living in and painting this shelter during the last Ice Age, when the climate was colder and drier, and continued to use it as conditions changed. This long occupation record shows the adaptability and resilience of Aboriginal people and their deep, enduring connection to country.

The shelter is protected as a significant cultural heritage site. Its art and archaeology provide invaluable insights into the long human history of Gariwerd and the rich cultural traditions of the Jardwadjali and Djab Wurrung peoples.

landmarks.name: Mudadgadjiin Shelter
landmarks.shortDescription: A rock art shelter in the Grampians with vivid ochre paintings recording thousands of years of culture.
landmarks.fullStory:
Mudadgadjiin Shelter is another important rock art site within the Grampians (Gariwerd) National Park. Like its neighbouring shelters, Mudadgadjiin preserves painted images created by the Jardwadjali and Djab Wurrung peoples over many generations.

The paintings at Mudadgadjiin include figures, symbols, and hand stencils applied in ochre pigments. Each painting was created with care and purpose — rock art was not casual decoration but a form of communication, ceremony, and record-keeping. The images encode Dreaming stories, mark territorial boundaries, record significant events, and transmit knowledge between generations.

The sandstone shelters of Gariwerd provided ideal surfaces for painting. The rock overhangs protected the art from rain and direct sunlight, allowing images to survive for thousands of years. The ochre pigments — made from grinding coloured minerals and mixing them with water or animal fat — bonded with the stone surface to create remarkably durable images.

Mudadgadjiin is part of the vast network of rock art sites across Gariwerd that together tell the story of one of the world's oldest continuing artistic traditions. These sites are sacred to the Jardwadjali and Djab Wurrung peoples and are protected under cultural heritage legislation.

landmarks.name: Djab Wurrung Heritage Trees
landmarks.shortDescription: Ancient scarred trees sacred to the Djab Wurrung people, some over 800 years old and connected to birthing traditions.
landmarks.fullStory:
The Djab Wurrung Heritage Trees, near Ararat in western Victoria, are a group of ancient trees that hold profound cultural and spiritual significance for the Djab Wurrung people. Some of these trees are estimated to be over 800 years old and carry the scars of bark removal for canoes, shields, and coolamons.

Among the most significant are the birthing trees — hollow trees where Djab Wurrung women traditionally gave birth. These trees are living connections to ancestors and to the continuous chain of life and culture. The placenta of each newborn was placed within the hollow of the tree, binding child and country together from the moment of birth.

The Djab Wurrung Heritage Trees became the focus of a major cultural heritage campaign when a highway duplication project threatened to destroy several of the most significant trees. Djab Wurrung people and supporters established a protest camp that lasted years, drawing national and international attention to the importance of protecting these living cultural sites.

The campaign highlighted the conflict between infrastructure development and cultural heritage protection, and the need for Aboriginal communities to have a meaningful voice in decisions that affect their sacred sites. The trees remain a powerful symbol of cultural resilience and the fight to protect irreplaceable heritage.

landmarks.name: Mount William Aboriginal Stone Axe Quarry
landmarks.shortDescription: Australia's largest known Aboriginal stone quarry, where prized greenstone axes were crafted for thousands of years.
landmarks.fullStory:
Mount William, near Lancefield in central Victoria, is the site of the largest known Aboriginal stone axe quarry in Australia. For thousands of years, the Wurundjeri people of the Kulin Nation mined a distinctive green-grey stone (metamorphosed sandstone) from outcrops on the mountain and crafted it into highly prized axes.

Mount William axes were among the most valued trade items in south-eastern Australia. They were exchanged across vast distances through intricate trade networks — axes from this quarry have been found hundreds of kilometres away. The trade was governed by strict protocols: the Wurundjeri controlled access to the quarry, and the exchange of axes strengthened alliances, settled disputes, and maintained relationships between language groups.

The quarry site preserves thousands of quarrying pits, stone flakes from axe production, and grinding grooves where rough blanks were shaped and polished. The scale of production indicates that Mount William was not just a local resource but a site of major economic and social importance across the region.

Mount William is listed on the Victorian Heritage Register and the National Heritage List. It stands as powerful evidence of the sophisticated economic systems, trade networks, and social structures that Aboriginal Australians developed over tens of thousands of years.

landmarks.name: Wurdi Youang Stone Arrangement
landmarks.shortDescription: A mysterious stone arrangement that may mark the setting positions of the sun at solstices and equinox.
landmarks.fullStory:
Wurdi Youang is an Aboriginal stone arrangement near Little River in Victoria, on the traditional country of the Wathaurong people. The arrangement consists of roughly 100 basalt stones set in an egg-shaped formation about 50 metres across, with its major axis aligned east-west.

Researchers have suggested that some of the prominent stones at Wurdi Youang may indicate the positions where the sun sets at the solstices and equinoxes. If confirmed, this would make it one of the oldest known astronomical observatories in the world, predating Stonehenge by thousands of years.

Aboriginal Australians were sophisticated astronomers. They used the stars, sun, and moon to navigate, to know when certain foods would be available, and to time ceremonies. The night sky was a map of stories — the Emu in the Sky, the Seven Sisters, and many other constellations told Dreaming stories that encoded practical knowledge about the natural world.

Wurdi Youang reminds us that Aboriginal knowledge systems were — and are — complex, precise, and deeply connected to the land and sky. The site is sacred to the Wathaurong people and is managed with their guidance and oversight.

landmarks.name: Kow Swamp
landmarks.shortDescription: One of the largest and most significant Aboriginal burial sites in Australia, dating back over 13,000 years.
landmarks.fullStory:
Kow Swamp, near Cohuna in northern Victoria, is one of the most significant Aboriginal burial sites in Australia. Archaeological excavations in the 1960s and 1970s uncovered the remains of over 40 individuals, dating from approximately 9,000 to 13,000 years ago — making it one of the largest collections of late Pleistocene human remains found anywhere in the world.

The burials at Kow Swamp revealed important information about the people who lived in the Murray River region during and after the last Ice Age. The remains showed evidence of cultural practices including cranial modification and the use of ochre in burial rituals, indicating sophisticated ceremonial traditions.

The excavation and study of these remains became a landmark case in the movement for the repatriation of Aboriginal ancestral remains. After years of campaigning by Aboriginal communities, the Kow Swamp remains were returned to the Traditional Owners in 1990 and reburied in a ceremony that honoured both the ancestors and the living culture that sought their return.

Kow Swamp stands as both an archaeological site of global significance and a powerful symbol of the Aboriginal fight for the respectful treatment of ancestral remains. The repatriation set an important precedent for Indigenous cultural heritage rights in Australia and internationally.

landmarks.name: Aboriginal Scarred Trees
landmarks.shortDescription: Living records of Aboriginal life — trees marked centuries ago when bark was cut for canoes, shields, and shelters.
landmarks.fullStory:
Across Victoria, ancient trees bear the scars of Aboriginal bark harvesting — oval or shield-shaped marks where large sheets of bark were carefully removed to make canoes, shields, coolamons (carrying vessels), shelters, and containers. These scarred trees are among the most widespread and visible forms of Aboriginal cultural heritage in the landscape.

Bark removal was a skilled practice governed by strict protocols. The right tree species had to be selected — typically river red gums, box eucalyptus, or grey box — and the bark removed at the right time of year, when sap flow made it pliable. Only one section was taken from each tree, allowing it to heal and continue growing. Many scarred trees are 200 to 500 years old, with some estimated at over 800 years.

The shape and size of the scar often reveals what the bark was used for. Long, narrow scars typically indicate canoe bark; smaller, more oval scars suggest shields or coolamons. The regrowth around each scar creates a distinctive raised border that becomes more pronounced as the tree ages.

Scarred trees are protected under the Aboriginal Heritage Act 2006 (Victoria). They are living cultural heritage — not artefacts in a museum, but markers in the landscape that connect the present to thousands of years of Aboriginal life, craft, and land management.

landmarks.name: Buchan Caves
landmarks.shortDescription: Ancient limestone caves in East Gippsland with deep cultural significance to the Gunaikurnai people.
landmarks.fullStory:
The Buchan Caves in East Gippsland, Victoria, are a network of ancient limestone caves formed over millions of years by underground rivers dissolving the rock. The caves and surrounding country are the traditional land of the Gunaikurnai people, who have lived in this region for tens of thousands of years.

For the Gunaikurnai, these caves and the surrounding landscape are deeply significant. The rivers, mountains, and forests of Gippsland feature in Dreaming stories that explain the creation of the land and guide the Gunaikurnai in their responsibilities as custodians. Archaeological evidence from the region shows that Aboriginal people inhabited the area during the last Ice Age, making it one of the longest-occupied regions in Australia.

The caves contain spectacular formations — stalactites, stalagmites, and flowstone created over hundreds of thousands of years. The surrounding bushland is rich with native plants and animals that sustained the Gunaikurnai through the seasons.

Today, the Gunaikurnai people have regained formal recognition of their connection to this country through native title. The caves are a place where geological time and cultural time meet — ancient stone formations alongside a living culture that stretches back to the deep past.

landmarks.name: Gippsland Lakes
landmarks.shortDescription: A vast coastal lagoon system on Gunaikurnai country, shaped by Dreaming stories and sustained by ancient knowledge.
landmarks.fullStory:
The Gippsland Lakes, in eastern Victoria, form the largest navigable inland waterway system in Australia. This network of lakes, marshes, and lagoons stretches over 70 kilometres along the coast and is the traditional country of the Gunaikurnai people, who have lived here and managed its resources for tens of thousands of years.

In Gunaikurnai Dreaming, the landscape of Gippsland was shaped by ancestral beings whose journeys created the rivers, lakes, and mountains. The lakes provided an extraordinarily rich food source — fish, eels, waterbirds, shellfish, and aquatic plants sustained large populations across the seasons. The Gunaikurnai developed sophisticated fishing techniques and managed the waterways through controlled burning of surrounding vegetation.

The lakes system was also a major meeting point for different Gunaikurnai clans. Seasonal gatherings brought people together for ceremony, trade, dispute resolution, and social events. The abundance of food resources made it possible to support large gatherings that could last for weeks.

Today, the Gunaikurnai people hold native title over much of the Gippsland region, including parts of the lakes system. They are actively involved in managing the lakes and surrounding lands, combining traditional ecological knowledge with contemporary conservation science to protect this unique environment.

landmarks.name: Tarra-Bulga National Park
landmarks.shortDescription: Ancient temperate rainforest on Gunaikurnai country, a remnant of forests that once covered Gippsland.
landmarks.fullStory:
Tarra-Bulga National Park, in the Strzelecki Ranges of South Gippsland, protects some of the last remaining temperate rainforest in Victoria. This ancient forest of mountain ash, myrtle beech, and tree ferns is the traditional country of the Gunaikurnai people, who have known and cared for these forests for thousands of years.

The Gunaikurnai had deep knowledge of the rainforest and its resources. The cool, wet forests provided materials for tools, shelter, and ceremony. Lyrebirds, wombats, possums, and other forest animals were important food sources, and the Gunaikurnai understood the seasonal patterns that governed when different resources were available.

The forests of the Strzelecki Ranges were devastated by European settlement — logging, clearing for farmland, and bushfires destroyed the vast majority of the original forest. Tarra-Bulga is one of the few places where remnants of the old-growth forest survive, making it ecologically precious as well as culturally significant.

The park's towering mountain ash trees, some over 60 metres tall, and its dense understorey of ferns and mosses create a cathedral-like atmosphere. Walking through Tarra-Bulga is a reminder of what Gippsland's forests looked like before European contact — and of the Gunaikurnai people who lived among them for millennia.

landmarks.name: Point Nepean
landmarks.shortDescription: A windswept headland where the Bunurong people gathered shellfish and held ceremonies for thousands of years.
landmarks.fullStory:
Point Nepean, at the tip of the Mornington Peninsula in Victoria, is a place of cultural significance to the Bunurong (Boon Wurrung) people, who have been the custodians of this coastal country for thousands of years. The headland and surrounding coastline provided rich resources — shellfish, fish, seals, and coastal plants — that sustained the Bunurong through the seasons.

Shell middens along the coast at Point Nepean contain the layered remains of thousands of meals, building up over centuries as families returned to favourite camping spots. Analysis of these middens reveals the sophisticated marine resource management practised by the Bunurong — they harvested different species at different times of year, ensuring sustainable use of coastal resources.

The dramatic landscape of Point Nepean — windswept heathlands, rocky shores, and sheltered bays — features in Bunurong Dreaming stories. The narrow entrance to Port Phillip Bay, which Point Nepean guards, is the subject of creation narratives that explain how the bay was formed and the responsibilities that come with caring for this waterway.

Point Nepean has had many uses since European contact — quarantine station, military fortification, and national park. Throughout all these changes, the Bunurong connection to country has endured. Today, the Bunurong Land Council works to ensure that Aboriginal cultural heritage at Point Nepean is recognised, protected, and shared.
